content:
# Luke Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 90-91 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_005_page_001.jpg, LUK_005_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And it came-to-pass, while the crowd was pressing-upon him and hearing the word of God, and he was standing beside the lake of Gennesaret,

**2.** and he saw two boats standing beside the lake; but the fishermen, having gone-away from them, were washing the nets.

**3.** And having embarked into one of the boats, which was Simon's, he asked him to put-out a little from the land; and having sat-down, he was teaching the crowds from the boat.

**4.** And when he ceased speaking, he said to Simon, "Put out into the deep, and let-down your nets for a catch."

**5.** And Simon answering said, "Master, through [the] whole night having labored, we took nothing; but upon your word I will let-down the nets."

**6.** And having done this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish; and their nets were breaking.

**7.** And they beckoned to [their] partners in the other boat, [that] having come, they should help them; and they came, and they filled both the boats, so-that they were sinking.

**8.** And Simon Peter, having seen [this], fell-down at the knees of Jesus, saying, "Depart from me, because I am a sinful man, Lord."

**9.** For astonishment had seized him, and all those with him, at the catch of the fish which they had taken;

**10.** and likewise also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon. And Jesus said to Simon, "Fear not; from now-on you will be catching men alive."

**11.** And having brought the boats to the land, leaving all-things, they followed him.

**12.** And it came-to-pass, while he [was] in one of the cities, and behold, a man full of leprosy; and having seen Jesus, having fallen upon [his] face, he begged him, saying, "Lord, if you will, you are-able to cleanse me."

**13.** And having stretched-out [his] hand, he touched him, saying, "I will; be cleansed." And immediately the leprosy departed from him.

**14.** And he charged him to tell no-one, "But having gone, show yourself to the priest, and offer concerning your cleansing just-as Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."

**15.** But the word concerning him was spreading-through more, and many crowds were coming-together to hear and to be healed from their sicknesses.

**16.** But he himself was withdrawing in the desolate-places and praying.

**17.** And it came-to-pass on one of the days, and he was teaching, and [there] were Pharisees and teachers-of-the-law sitting-by, who had come out-of every village of Galilee and Judea and Jerusalem; and [the] power of [the] Lord was [present] for him to heal.

**18.** And behold, men carrying upon a bed a man who was having-been-paralyzed, and they were seeking to bring him in and to place [him] before him.

**19.** And not finding by what-way they might bring him in on-account-of the crowd, having gone-up upon the housetop, through the tiles they let him down with the small-bed into the midst, before Jesus.

**20.** And having seen their faith, he said, "Man, your sins have been forgiven you."

**21.** And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, "Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who is-able to forgive sins except God alone?"

**22.** But Jesus, having known their reasonings, answering said to them, "What are-you-reasoning in your hearts?

**23.** Which is easier, to say, 'Your sins have been forgiven you,' or to say, 'Rise and walk'?

**24.** But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority upon the earth to forgive sins" — he said to the [one] having-been-paralyzed — "I say to you, rise, and having taken-up your small-bed, go to your house."

**25.** And immediately having stood-up before them, having taken-up that-upon-which he was lying, he went-away to his house, glorifying God.

**26.** And ecstasy seized all, and they were glorifying God, and they were filled with fear, saying, "We have seen extraordinary-things today."

**27.** And after these-things he went-out, and he beheld a tax-collector named Levi, sitting at the tax-office, and he said to him, "Follow me."

**28.** And having left all-things, rising-up, he was following him.

**29.** And Levi made a great reception for him in his house; and there was a large crowd of tax-collectors and others who were reclining [at table] with them.

**30.** And the Pharisees and their scribes were murmuring against his disciples, saying, "Why with the tax-collectors and sinners do-you-eat and drink?"

**31.** And Jesus answering said to them, "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are ill.

**32.** I have not come to call righteous [ones] but sinners to repentance."

**33.** And they said to him, "The disciples of John fast often and make prayers, and likewise [those] of the Pharisees; but yours eat and drink."

**34.** And Jesus said to them, "You are not able to make the sons of the bridal-chamber fast while the bridegroom is with them?

**35.** But days will come, and when the bridegroom is taken-away from them, then they will fast in those days."

**36.** And he was also speaking a parable to them: "No-one tears a piece from a new garment [and] puts [it] upon an old garment; but if indeed [he does], both the new [one] he will tear, and with the old [one] the piece from the new will not agree.

**37.** And no-one puts new wine into old wineskins; but if indeed [he does], the new wine will burst the wineskins, and it will be poured-out, and the wineskins will be destroyed.

**38.** But new wine must be put into new wineskins.

**39.** And no-one having drunk old [wine] desires new; for he says, 'The old is good.'"

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **5:1 — "Gennesaret"**: Sinaiticus reads ΓΕΝΝΗΣΑΡΕΤ (Gennesaret), the standard form for the Lake of Galilee's western shore region.

2. **5:2 — "the nets" (τα δικτυα)**: Sinaiticus uses δικτυα (nets) here, not the more specific σαγηνη (dragnet) or αμφιβληστρον (casting-net).

3. **5:6 — "breaking" (διερρησσετο)**: Sinaiticus reads the imperfect tense, indicating the nets were in the process of breaking — not that they had already torn apart.

4. **5:8 — "Simon Peter"**: Sinaiticus includes the double name Σιμων Πετρος here. Some later manuscripts read only "Simon." This is notably the first use of "Peter" in Luke's narrative.

5. **5:10 — "catching men alive" (ζωγρων)**: The Greek ζωγρεω literally means "to capture alive." EMET preserves the vivid metaphor from Sinaiticus rather than softening to "catching men."

6. **5:14 — Tell no-one**: The command structure in Sinaiticus runs directly from the charge to tell no-one into the quoted instruction beginning with "But having gone..." — the syntax is compressed.

7. **5:15 — "was spreading-through more" (διηρχετο μαλλον)**: Sinaiticus reads the imperfect of διερχομαι with μαλλον (more/rather), indicating an intensifying spread of his fame.

8. **5:17 — "for him to heal"**: Sinaiticus reads αυτον with the infinitive ιασθαι, yielding "for him to heal" — that is, the Lord's power was present enabling Jesus to heal. Some manuscripts read αυτους ("them" — i.e., to heal them).

9. **5:19 — "through the tiles" (δια των κεραμων)**: Luke's distinctive detail. Sinaiticus preserves δια των κεραμων, reflecting a Greco-Roman tiled roof, whereas Mark 2:4 describes digging through a mud roof. This is not a contradiction but reflects Luke's audience.

10. **5:26 — "ecstasy" (εκστασις)**: Sinaiticus reads εκστασις, literally "a standing-outside-oneself." EMET renders this as "ecstasy" in its original Greek sense of astonishment and displacement of mind, not modern emotional connotations.

11. **5:30 — "their scribes"**: Sinaiticus reads οι γραμματεις αυτων — "their scribes," i.e., the scribes belonging to the Pharisees' party. Some manuscripts omit αυτων.

12. **5:33 — "yours" (οι σοι)**: Sinaiticus uses the compressed expression "the yours" (οι σοι) meaning "your [disciples]," without repeating μαθηται. EMET preserves this brevity.

13. **5:36 — Parable of the patch**: Sinaiticus preserves the fuller form: "both the new he will tear, and with the old the piece from the new will not agree" — a double consequence not always retained in later manuscripts.

14. **5:39 — "good" (χρηστος)**: Sinaiticus reads χρηστος ("good/pleasant") rather than the Byzantine variant χρηστοτερος ("better"). This is a significant variant: Sinaiticus simply says the old is "good," not "better." Some manuscripts omit verse 39 entirely.

15. **5:39 — Verse inclusion**: This entire verse is present in Sinaiticus. It is absent from Codex Bezae (D), leading some scholars to question its originality. Its presence in Aleph supports its inclusion.

16. **5:20 — "Man" (ανθρωπε)**: Sinaiticus addresses the paralytic as ανθρωπε (vocative, "Man" / "O man"), a direct and somewhat formal address that EMET preserves literally.

17. **5:32 — "to repentance" (εις μετανοιαν)**: Sinaiticus includes εις μετανοιαν. This phrase is absent in some Western text witnesses, but Sinaiticus supports its presence.
